context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 4th March, 2024
Days Covered: 06 and 07 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Asymptotic Relationships [15 points]

(a) [7 pts] For each pair of functions f : ℕ+ → ℝ+ and g : ℕ+ → ℝ+ in the table below, list in the third column which of the following is the strongest or most specific asymptotic relationship that holds between f and g, from among these options:
    f ∼ g,   f ∈ Θ(g),   f ∈ O(g),   f ∈ o(g),   f ∈ ω(g),   f ∈ Ω(g),   None.
You do not need to explain your work in this part. (Assume that log means log2 if no base is specified.)

| f(n)                       | g(n)                       | Relationship? |
|----------------------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| 6^n                        | n^1200                     |               |
| (log n)^6                  | 1200th-root of n           |               |
| log_6(n^1200)              | (log_1200 n)^6             |               |
| log(n!)                    | n log n                    |               |
| 2^⌈log2 n⌉                 | 3^⌈log3 n⌉                 |               |
| 2^⌈log2 n⌉ − n + 1         | 3^⌈log3 n⌉ − n + 1         |               |
| n                          | n^(cos log n)              |               |

(b) [8 pts] Answer the same question for the pairs below, and justify your reasoning.

| f(n)             | g(n)             | Relationship? |
|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 2^((log n)^2)    | 2^(⌈log n⌉^2)    |               |
| 4^n              | (2n)! / (n!)^2   |               |


Problem 2. OEIS [10 points]

(a) [2 pts] Consider the sequence that begins with x0 = 0, x1 = 1, x2 = 2, and continues with xn = 5·x_{n−2} + 2·x_{n−3} for n ≥ 3. What are the values of x3, x4, x5, x6?
(b) [1 pt] Let's practice one of the most useful techniques when investigating a sequence or recurrence: checking whether someone else has already done it for you! The OEIS (Online Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences) is an invaluable tool that houses more than 300,000 noteworthy integer sequences together with descriptions, formulas, external references, relationships to other OEIS entries, etc.
Use https://oeis.org/ to look up an entry that might possibly match our sequence from part (a): type the values we've computed into their search bar, separated by commas. If you're not sure you've found the right one, you can gain confidence by computing more terms using our recurrence and seeing if they continue to match.
(c) [1 pt] Browse your chosen entry (scroll down to the "Formula" section) to find a possible closed form function of n that equals xn. Here, closed form means that the function can be computed directly from n without relying on other terms in the sequence or iterative processes such as Σ, Π, or "dot-dot-dot"s. Basic arithmetic functions such as exponents are fine.
    Note: lines that include the acronyms "g.f." or "e.g.f." (generating function and exponential generating function) can be safely ignored for this problem.
(d) [6 pts] Don't trust everything you read online! Use induction to carefully prove the formula you found in the previous part.


Problem 3. Bounded Asymptotics [15 points]

In this problem, we investigate the asymptotic relationship of functions under some conditions. You can assume that any function mentioned in the problem maps ℕ to ℝ+. You might find the following definitions useful:
1. A function f is bounded from below (respectively above) if there exists a real number B such that f(n) ≥ B (respectively f(n) ≤ B) for all n ∈ ℕ.
2. A function f is bounded away from zero if there exists a real number B > 0 such that f(n) ≥ B for all n ∈ ℕ.
3. A function f is strictly increasing (respectively decreasing) if for any a, b ∈ ℕ we have that a > b ⇒ f(a) > f(b) (respectively a > b ⇒ f(a) < f(b)).

(a) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is bounded from above and g be a function that is bounded away from zero. Carefully prove that f ∈ O(g).
    Hint: Careful! The limit lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) might not exist, so your argument shouldn't use limits.
(b) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is bounded from above and g be a function that is bounded from below. Is it necessarily true that f ∈ O(g)?
(c) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is strictly decreasing and g be a function that is strictly increasing. Is it necessarily true that f ∈ O(g)? Is it necessarily true that f ∈ o(g)?


Problem 4. Karatsuba's Algorithm [10 points]

As we'll see soon, cryptography often requires arithmetic with large integers, possibly with hundreds or thousands of digits each! When adding two integers A and B each with n digits, the straightforward algorithm requires at most 2n single-digit additions. But the straightforward multiplication algorithm involves multiplying each digit of A with each digit of B, which is already Θ(n^2) operations. Karatsuba's Algorithm is a clever divide-and-conquer algorithm with (hopefully) faster asymptotic runtime.

Karatsuba's Algorithm can multiply two n-digit numbers using at most T(n) single-digit arithmetic operations, where T(n) is defined by the following recurrence:
    T(0) = 0,   T(1) = 1,   T(n) = 3·T(⌈n/2⌉) + 10n   for n ≥ 2.

(a) [1 pt] Compute T(2), T(3), and T(4).
(b) [4 pts] Use the Plug and Chug method to find an exact (not just asymptotic!) closed-form expression for T(n), assuming n is an exact power of 2. You do not need to prove your formula, but you should check it against T(1), T(2), and T(4).
    Note: We'd like you to use the Plug and Chug method specifically; solutions that arrive at a closed form using other techniques (or without explanation) will only receive partial credit.
(c) [4 pts] Karatsuba's algorithm works whether or not n is a power of 2, and the same recurrence applies, so let's find an approximate solution for T(n) in this more general case: carefully use the Master Theorem to prove an asymptotic bound of the form T(n) ∈ Θ(g(n)), for some closed-form function g(n).
(d) [1 pt] Is Karatsuba an asymptotic improvement over the straightforward Θ(n^2) algorithm? Why or why not?

Optional Note: Here's a rough description of Karatsuba's Algorithm, in case you're curious. If A and B are n-digit numbers, we can split each list of digits in half by writing A = A0·X + A1 and B = B0·X + B1, where X = 10^⌈n/2⌉. We could compute A · B using the formula
    A · B = [A0·B0]·X^2 + ([A0·B1] + [A1·B0])·X + [A1·B1],
which requires 4 multiplications of ⌈n/2⌉-digit numbers, leading to T(n) = 4·T(⌈n/2⌉) + O(n) with solution Θ(n^2). Karatsuba cleverly observed that P, Q, R can be computed with just three multiplications:
    P = [A0·B0]
    R = [A1·B1]
    Q = P + R − [(A0 − A1)·(B0 − B1)].
This reduces the 4 to a 3 in the recurrence, while the O(n) term remains O(n), which directly leads to the asymptotic speedup.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem Set 4, especially the asymptotics parts. Could we start really broad—like a big-picture explanation of how I’m even supposed to compare these functions?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's start there. Before I say anything, what do you already know about what it means for one function to be asymptotically 'smaller' or 'larger' than another? Even an informal sense of it is a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation before engaging with any specific problem. Rather than lecturing, I should use the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge and guide them to articulate what they already know. I'll ask a targeted question to get them thinking about the core concept themselves, keeping my response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Honestly not much beyond “which one grows faster,” and then I mix up \(O\), \(\Theta\), \(o\), and \(\omega\). I kind of want the whole overview first because every row in Problem 1 looks different to me.
exchanges.tutor: That informal sense is actually the right foundation! So let's build on it. You have six relationships: f ~ g, Θ, O, o, Ω, ω. If 'grows faster' is your core idea, can you sort these into two camps: ones that say f grows *no faster* than g, and ones that say f grows *at least as fast* as g? Take a guess — even partial is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad overview before tackling specific problems. While it's tempting to give a lecture, the Socratic method calls for drawing out what they already know. Their informal sense of 'which grows faster' is actually the right intuition - I should build on that and ask them to connect it to the specific notations, rather than explaining everything myself. I'll keep it bite-sized and focused.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure, and when I guess I usually flip them. Could you just give me the full cheat-sheet version first, like what each symbol means and how they relate, before I try sorting them?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but handing you a cheat sheet isn't my role here - working through it together is how it actually sticks. Your textbook has the formal definitions if you need a reference. Here's a simpler entry point: just pick *one* symbol, say O. In your own words, what do you think f ∈ O(g) means, even loosely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to just give them a cheat sheet, which would bypass the learning process. I should decline to do that and instead use a gentler Socratic push - pointing them to their textbook and asking a more targeted question that makes the sorting task feel more manageable. I'll acknowledge their frustration but hold firm on not just delivering the answer.

exchanges.student: Maybe it means \(f\) is smaller than \(g\) eventually, but I’m not confident because sometimes constants don’t matter and sometimes they do. That didn’t really clear it up for me, so maybe I should switch angles: can I just tell you which rows in 1(a) feel impossible to compare and get a broad explanation for those types?
exchanges.tutor: You're close! O is less about 'smaller' and more about 'bounded above up to a constant.' So f ∈ O(g) means there exists some constant c such that f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for all large enough n. Now, with that in mind: what would f ∈ Ω(g) mean, by analogy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to redirect the conversation to specific rows, which is actually progress - but they haven't yet nailed down the basic definitions. Their intuition about O is close but imprecise. I should affirm what's right, gently correct the imprecision, and then guide them to sharpen their own definition before jumping to specific rows. I want to avoid letting them skip the foundation entirely.

exchanges.student: I guess bounded below by a constant times \(g\) eventually. I’m still not seeing the whole landscape though, and I keep losing track of which ones are strict vs non-strict, so I’d really like the all-at-once picture instead of one symbol at a time.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on Ω — good. You now have O and Ω. Notice that Θ just means *both* O and Ω hold simultaneously. So what would be your best guess at the difference between O and o — what makes o the 'strict' version?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived Ω from O by analogy, which is real progress. They're asking for the 'all at once' picture, but going symbol by symbol is actually the right pedagogical approach - and they've now got two symbols down. I should acknowledge their correct answer, and then nudge them to derive the remaining ones themselves using the same analogy pattern. This keeps them doing the intellectual work rather than receiving a lecture. I should not give a cheat sheet.

exchanges.student: Is it that \(o\) means \(f\) is not just at most a constant times \(g\), but actually much smaller eventually? I’m kind of spinning my wheels, so maybe could you show it through one concrete row, like \(6^n\) vs \(n^{1200}\), and then maybe one weird log row too?
exchanges.tutor: Spot on for o. And by the same analogy, ω is to Ω what o is to O — f grows strictly faster than g, not just at least as fast. You now have all the symbols. So let's apply them: for 6^n vs n^1200, which one do you think grows faster as n gets large, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified what little-o means - that's genuine progress and I should affirm it. They now have O, Ω, Θ, and o. ω follows by analogy. Rather than working through examples for them, I should close the loop on the definitions (just ω left) and then let them attempt the 6^n vs n^1200 row themselves. I should not work through examples for them - that would violate the 'never give the answer' rule. I'll note we've been on definitions for a while and push toward applying them.

exchanges.student: I think \(6^n\) grows faster than \(n^{1200}\), so maybe \(6^n \in \omega(n^{1200})\). That part feels guessable, but the rows with ceilings and the \(n^{\cos \log n}\) one make me want a more general “how to recognize the pattern” explanation.
exchanges.tutor: Correct! Now tell me *why* 6^n dominates n^1200. If you can articulate that, you'll have your own 'general pattern' for exponential vs polynomial comparisons — which covers more rows than you might think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the relationship for row 1. I should affirm this and ask them to articulate *why* (exponential vs polynomial), since that reasoning IS the general pattern they're asking for. Rather than giving a general lecture, I can get them to derive the principle themselves from this example. This is more effective than a top-down explanation.

exchanges.student: I only know the slogan that exponentials beat polynomials, not a proof idea. That approach isn’t really clicking for me, so I want to try a different angle: could I get a compact rule list for common growth classes—logs, powers of logs, roots/polynomials, exponentials, factorial-ish stuff—and then I’ll try mapping the rows onto it?
exchanges.tutor: The slogan 'exponentials beat polynomials' is actually enough reasoning for row 1 — you don't need a proof here. You've got the definitions and that hierarchy intuition. Rather than more groundwork, let's actually move: pick the next row that feels most approachable — (log n)^6 vs n^(1/1200) — and make a guess. Which grows faster?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a top-down explanation/cheat sheet rather than engaging with specific problems. I've been Socratic for several exchanges, and they've actually made good progress: they know all six definitions and correctly identified row 1. Rather than giving a lecture, I should redirect them to try applying what they know to the next rows, and note that the 'slogan' they already have IS enough to handle several rows. I should also note we've been on this meta-level discussion too long and push them toward actually attempting the problems.

exchanges.student: I’d guess the root \(n^{1/1200}\) grows faster than \((\log n)^6\), so \((\log n)^6 \in o(n^{1/1200})\). I’m still feeling shaky overall though; that didn’t work for making me feel less lost, so I want to switch topics for a second—Problem 2 also confuses me, and I kind of want a broad recap of how recurrences, closed forms, and induction are supposed to fit together.
exchanges.tutor: Correct again on row 2! You're doing better than you think — two rows down, and your reasoning is solid. Let's finish 1(a) before switching topics; jumping around tends to leave everything half-done. Row 3: log_6(n^1200) vs (log_1200(n))^6 — what can you simplify each expression to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct about row 2 and should be affirmed. However, they're now trying to jump to Problem 2 before finishing Problem 1. This is a pattern of avoiding the work by seeking broad overviews. I should acknowledge their correct answer, but hold them to finishing what we started on Problem 1 before switching topics. We've made real progress on 1(a) - two rows down. I should keep them focused rather than letting them jump around.

exchanges.student: I know change-of-base probably turns both into constants times powers of \(\log n\), but I don’t trust myself to simplify them cleanly. Could you just do this row and maybe the ceiling one as model examples, because seeing two fully worked comparisons would probably help me more than me guessing again?
exchanges.tutor: I won't work through examples for you — that's your job, and honestly you're closer than you think. You already said the right thing: change-of-base turns these into constants times powers of log n. Just try simplifying *one side* first: what does log_6(n^1200) reduce to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to work out examples for them, which I must refuse. However, they do have the right instinct about change-of-base - I should affirm that and push them to actually apply it. I should not work out examples. Their instinct is correct and I should build confidence by asking them to just try the simplification of one side, not both at once.

deductions.reason: In Turn 4, the tutor gives the formal definition of O directly ('f ∈ O(g) means there exists some constant c such that f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for all large enough n') without first diagnosing what the student already understood or asking a diagnostic question to locate the confusion. The student had just given a partial answer and the tutor jumped to providing the definition rather than probing further.

The tutor never provides method-level feedback on the student's approach or thinking process. When the student gets answers correct (rows 1 and 2), the tutor simply confirms correctness and moves on without commenting on the reasoning method or how to generalize it. No feedback is given on the student's weak strategy of wanting cheat sheets rather than building understanding.

The student repeatedly requests a 'big picture overview,' 'cheat sheet,' 'rule list,' or 'model examples' across turns 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, and 10. The tutor keeps cycling through the same pattern of asking the student to guess the next symbol or row without consolidating progress or reframing the approach to address the student's persistent confusion. The student remains stuck in the same loop without the tutor synthesizing what has been learned or switching strategy meaningfully.

In Turn 9, the student explicitly asks to switch to Problem 2 ('I want to switch topics for a second—Problem 2 also confuses me'). The tutor appropriately redirects back to Problem 1, so no deduction is warranted here.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at avoiding giving away answers and maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, never producing submission-ready work. The student-led guessing is preserved and the tutor correctly validates student answers. However, the tutor falls into a repetitive pattern across many turns without adapting strategy when the student remains persistently confused, triggering a redundancy deduction. The tutor also jumps to providing the formal O definition in Turn 4 without sufficient diagnostic questioning. Meta-learning feedback is absent throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gives direct answers, rewrites student text, or reveals conclusions before student reasoning. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in Turn 4 the tutor provides the formal definition of Big-O directly after a partial student answer without first diagnosing what the student already understood. 1.3 loses 2 points because the tutor never provides process-level or method-level feedback; confirmations like 'Correct!' or 'Spot on' are given without any coaching on how the student should approach these problems more systematically. 2.1 loses 4 points because the student makes the same request for an overview/cheat sheet/rule list across at least 6 turns and the tutor cycles through the same question-per-symbol pattern without consolidating, reframing, or switching strategy to break the loop. 2.2 retains full marks as the tutor appropriately redirects off-topic drift in Turn 9. Communication quality is strong throughout with concise, clear responses.
